# Vectors {#sec-vectors}

```{r}
#| include: false
source(here::here("_common.R"))
```

Type `42` in R.

```{r}
42
```

That `[1]` has been there since Chapter 1, quietly announcing something you probably didn't notice: you never made a number. You made a vector.


## There are no scalars {#sec-no-scalars}

Most languages draw a hard line between a single number and a collection of numbers, treating them as fundamentally different creatures that live in different boxes and obey different rules. R refuses. Every value in R is a vector: `42` is a numeric vector of length 1, `"hello"` is a character vector of length 1, and `TRUE` is a logical vector of length 1.

```{r}
length(42)
length("hello")
length(TRUE)
```

Why would a language erase the distinction between "one thing" and "many things"? Because S (@sec-s-language), the language R descends from, was built for people who work with columns of data, not individual numbers. A single temperature reading is rarely interesting on its own; you care about the whole column. So S made the column the basic unit of computation, and R inherited that decision wholesale, which means every operation you write, from arithmetic to comparison, has to reckon with that choice.

`x * 2` works the same whether `x` has one element or a million, because `x` is always a vector and R doesn't need a special case for scalars; it just operates on vectors. But how do you build them?


## Atomic vectors {#sec-atomic-vectors}

You make a vector with `c()`, short for "combine":

```{r}
temperatures <- c(72, 85, 61, 90, 78)
temperatures
```

`c()` can combine vectors too:

```{r}
morning <- c(58, 62, 55)
afternoon <- c(75, 80, 71)
all_temps <- c(morning, afternoon)
all_temps
```

It flattens everything into a single vector; there are no nested vectors in R, so `c(c(1, 2), c(3, 4))` gives you `c(1, 2, 3, 4)`. Look at what's happening in `c(morning, afternoon)`:

```
c(morning, afternoon)
= c(c(58, 62, 55), c(75, 80, 71))
= c(58, 62, 55, 75, 80, 71)
= [58, 62, 55, 75, 80, 71]
```

Each step replaces a name or function call with its value. `morning` becomes `c(58, 62, 55)`, the nested `c()` calls flatten into one, and the last step is where term replacement ends and the computer takes over: R allocates a contiguous block of memory, stores six numbers side by side, and the expression has been reduced to a value that lives in memory. This is term replacement, the logic at the heart of Church's lambda calculus (@sec-church): you have an expression, you replace each piece with its value until nothing is left to replace. In Chapter 1, `add(3, add(5, 10))` reduced step by step until only `18` remained. Here, `c(morning, afternoon)` reduces to a six-element vector. Even building a data structure is a function call that reduces to a value. (Term replacement is one of the oldest ideas in logic and a cornerstone of computer science. Mathematicians were studying it decades before electronic computers existed; it turns out that "replace an expression with its value" is enough to define all of computation.)

::: callout-note
## For the curious
`c()` has two properties worth noticing. First, it is *associative*: `c(c(1, 2), c(3, 4))` and `c(1, c(2, c(3, 4)))` both produce `c(1, 2, 3, 4)`. Grouping doesn't matter. Second, `c(x, NULL)` returns `x` unchanged, so `NULL` acts as an *identity element*: combining with it does nothing. An operation that is associative and has an identity element is called a *monoid*. Addition with 0 is a monoid. String concatenation with `""` is a monoid. `c()` with `NULL` is a monoid. The pattern keeps showing up: dplyr pipelines (@sec-data-transformation), function composition (@sec-pipes-and-composition), and `Reduce()` (@sec-reduce-and-accumulate) all rely on it.
:::

A few shortcuts for common patterns:

```{r}
1:5
```

```{r}
seq(0, 1, by = 0.25)
```

```{r}
rep("hello", 3)
```

`1:5` generates integers from 1 to 5, `seq()` generates a sequence with a specified step size, and `rep()` repeats a value.

R has four atomic types you will use constantly, and every one of them follows a pecking order that R enforces behind your back. Two more exist but rarely matter:

```{r}
typeof(3.14)
typeof(42L)
typeof("hello")
typeof(TRUE)
```

`double` is a decimal number (even `42` is stored as a double by default), while `integer` needs an `L` suffix: `42L`. `character` is text, `logical` is `TRUE` or `FALSE`. The other two types, `complex` and `raw`, exist for specialized work; you can ignore them.

Every element in a vector must be the same type. `typeof()` and `length()` answer the two most basic questions about any vector: what type are the elements, and how many are there? But what happens when you try to break that same-type rule?


### Exercises {.unnumbered}

1. What does `c(1, c(2, c(3, 4)))` produce? Predict, then check.
2. Create a vector of the first 10 even numbers using `seq()`.
3. What is `typeof(1)`? What about `typeof(1L)`?


## Coercion {#sec-coercion}

Try mixing types in a vector:

```{r}
c(1, "two", 3)
```

R converted the numbers to text. No warning, no error. This is called *coercion*, and R follows a fixed hierarchy: logical < integer < double < character. When you mix types, everything gets promoted to the most general one.

```{r}
c(TRUE, 1, "hello")
```

`TRUE` became `"1"` (first promoted to the integer 1, then to the string `"1"`), and `1` became `"1"`.

This hierarchy explains a trick from Chapter 1 that might have seemed like magic. `sum(scores > 70)` works because `scores > 70` produces a logical vector, and `sum()` coerces the logicals to numbers: `TRUE` becomes 1, `FALSE` becomes 0, so the count of passing values falls out automatically:

```{r}
x <- c(65, 82, 71, 90, 55, 78)
x > 70
sum(x > 70)
```

::: callout-tip
## Opinion
R will never warn you about coercion. `c(1, 2, "3")` quietly becomes all character, and your arithmetic breaks ten lines later with an error message that points nowhere near the real problem. Memorize the hierarchy: logical < integer < double < character. It will save you hours.
:::


### Exercises {.unnumbered}

1. What does `TRUE + 1` return? What about `FALSE * 5`?
2. What is `typeof(c(1L, 1.5))`? Which type won?
3. What does `as.numeric("hello")` return? Read the warning.


## Vectorized operations {#sec-vectorized-ops}

Suppose you need to convert five temperatures from Fahrenheit to Celsius. In most languages, you write a loop: go through each element, apply the formula, store the result. In R:

```{r}
temperatures <- c(72, 85, 61, 90, 78)
(temperatures - 32) * 5/9
```

One expression, no loop. The subtraction, multiplication, and division each apply to every element at once. This is a *vectorized operation*, and it is the default way of working in R.

Comparisons are vectorized too:

```{r}
temperatures > 75
```

The result is a logical vector of the same length: `TRUE` where the condition holds, `FALSE` where it doesn't.

You can combine comparisons with `&` (and) and `|` (or):

```{r}
temperatures > 60 & temperatures < 85
```

Arithmetic, comparisons, and most built-in functions all work this way, returning a vector of the same length for each input vector. `sqrt()`, `log()`, `round()`, `abs()`: give them a vector and they return a vector.

```{r}
sqrt(c(1, 4, 9, 16, 25))
```

In lambda calculus (@sec-church), `sqrt` is a function `f = λx. √x`. Even `√` itself is not a primitive; it can be computed by repeated function application. You start with a rough guess and improve it over and over until it's close enough:

```
√x = iterate(λguess. (guess + x / guess) / 2, x)
```

Start with a guess, apply the refinement function `λguess. (guess + x / guess) / 2` repeatedly, and the result converges to `√x`. Square root reduces to division, addition, and repeated application of a lambda, which are themselves reducible further. At every level, the building blocks are functions applied to arguments.

::: callout-note
## Under the hood
In practice, R calls C's `sqrt()`, which on modern CPUs maps to a hardware instruction. The chip uses a lookup table for a rough estimate, then refines it with the same `(guess + x / guess) / 2` step, finishing in 10-20 clock cycles. The abstraction and the silicon converge on the same algorithm.
:::

For our purposes, `f = λx. √x` is enough. R applies `f` to each element by term replacement:

```
f = λx. √x

f(c(1, 4, 9, 16, 25))
= c(f(1), f(4), f(9), f(16), f(25))          # distribute f over the vector
= c((λx. √x)(1), (λx. √x)(4), ..., (λx. √x)(25))  # replace f with its definition
= c(√1, √4, √9, √16, √25)                   # substitute each x
= c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
```

R distributes the function over the vector automatically; you write one application, R performs five. This is Church's model extended to collections: a function applied to a vector produces a vector of results. `sqrt(c(1, 4, 9, 16, 25))` is a single function applied to a single argument, and the fact that the argument contains five numbers is R's business, not yours.

::: callout-note
## For the curious
This pattern has a name: a *functor*. A functor maps a function over a structure while preserving the structure's shape. `sqrt` applied to a five-element vector returns a five-element vector; the function changes the contents, but the container stays intact. Vectors are functors, lists are functors (via `lapply`), and data frame columns are functors (via `mutate`). The word comes from category theory, but the idea is concrete: apply inside, preserve outside. You will see it everywhere in this book, and by the end it will feel as natural as vectorization itself.
:::


### Recycling {#sec-recycling}

What happens when you operate on two vectors of different lengths?

```{r}
c(1, 2, 3, 4) + c(10, 20)
```

R *recycled* the shorter vector, reusing `10, 20` to match the length of the longer one: `1+10, 2+20, 3+10, 4+20`. This is also how `temperatures - 32` works: `32` is a vector of length 1, and R recycles it five times to match the length of `temperatures`.

When the lengths don't divide evenly, R warns you:

```{r}
c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) + c(10, 20)
```

::: callout-tip
## Opinion
Recycling is useful when the shorter vector has length 1 (e.g., `x * 2`, `x > 0`). For anything else, match your vector lengths explicitly. A recycling warning almost always means a bug.
:::


### Exercises {.unnumbered}

1. What does `c(1, 2, 3) * c(0, 1)` produce? Does R warn you?
2. Why does `x - mean(x)` work without a loop? (Hint: what length is `mean(x)`?)
3. What does `c(TRUE, FALSE) + c(1, 2, 3, 4)` produce?


## Indexing {#sec-indexing}

You often need specific elements from a vector, and the `[` operator extracts them. There are four ways to use it.

**By position:**

```{r}
temperatures <- c(72, 85, 61, 90, 78)
temperatures[1]
temperatures[c(1, 3, 5)]
```

**By exclusion** (negative indices):

```{r}
temperatures[-2]
```

Everything except the second element.

**By logical vector:**

```{r}
temperatures[temperatures > 75]
```

`temperatures > 75` produces `c(FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE)`, and `[` keeps only the `TRUE` elements. You're asking a yes/no question of every element and keeping the ones that pass. This is filtering, and it will come back in a more powerful form when you learn `dplyr::filter()` (@sec-data-transformation).

```{r}
hot <- temperatures > 75
temperatures[hot]
```

**By name:**

```{r}
scores <- c(alice = 92, bob = 78, carol = 85)
scores["bob"]
scores[c("alice", "carol")]
```

You can also use `[` to replace elements:

```{r}
x <- c(10, 20, 30)
x[2] <- 99
x
```

This looks like it's changing the vector in place, but R is actually making a copy with the new value and pointing the name `x` at the copy. The old vector (with 20 in position 2) is gone. This is copy-on-modify, the same idea from @sec-names-binding: values in R don't change; names get moved to point at new values. So far, every element in our vectors has been present and accounted for. Real data is not so tidy.


### Exercises {.unnumbered}

1. Extract the last element of `temperatures` without hard-coding the position. (Hint: `length()`.)
2. What does `temperatures[c(TRUE, FALSE)]` return? How does recycling interact with logical indexing?
3. Create a named vector of three cities and their populations. Extract two by name.


## Missing values {#sec-missing-values}

A sensor fails, a patient skips a visit, a field is left blank. Real data has gaps, and R represents them with `NA` ("Not Available"):

```{r}
readings <- c(23.1, NA, 22.5, 24.0, NA, 23.8)
readings
```

`NA` is not zero, and it is not an empty string. It means "I don't know." And that uncertainty is contagious: any computation involving an unknown value produces an unknown result.

```{r}
sum(readings)
mean(readings)
```

R is being honest. If you don't know some of the values, you don't know the sum. If you want to ignore the missing values, you have to say so explicitly:

```{r}
sum(readings, na.rm = TRUE)
mean(readings, na.rm = TRUE)
```

`na.rm = TRUE` means "remove NAs before computing." Many R functions have this argument.

Find which elements are missing with `is.na()`:

```{r}
is.na(readings)
```

Filter them out with `!` (not):

```{r}
readings[!is.na(readings)]
```

One common trap: you cannot test for `NA` with `==`.

```{r}
NA == NA
```

This returns `NA`, not `TRUE`. Think about it: if you don't know what `x` is and you don't know what `y` is, you can't know whether they're equal. Always use `is.na()`.

This contagious behavior is not arbitrary; R is implementing a discipline where a value is either present or absent, and any operation on an absent value produces absence. The principle is older than R.

Haskell, a language that emerged in 1990 when a group of functional programming researchers decided to consolidate their dozens of incompatible dialects, takes the strictest possible position: no variable can ever change its value, and no function can have a side effect. The compiler enforces these rules, which means the type system carries more information than in most languages. (The language is named after Haskell Curry, a logician who worked on combinatory logic in the 1930s and 1940s; he will reappear in @sec-programs-and-proofs.)

One consequence: in Haskell, the possibility of absence is built into the type system as `Maybe`. A value is either `Just 42` or `Nothing`, and any operation on `Nothing` propagates `Nothing` forward. `1 + NA` returning `NA` in R is the same logic as Haskell's `Nothing >>= f` returning `Nothing`.

Rust adopted the same idea for different reasons. Graydon Hoare started Rust at Mozilla in 2006 because C and C++ gave you direct control over memory but no protection against the bugs that come with it: buffer overflows, use-after-free, data races. He borrowed heavily from Haskell's type system, and Rust's equivalent of `Maybe` is called `Option`: a value is `Some(42)` or `None`, and `None.map(f)` returns `None`. The compiler rejects any code that forgets to check whether a value is present.

R's approach is looser: nothing stops you from ignoring `NA`, and you will not find out until your results look wrong. But the underlying rule is the same. If any input is unknown, the output is unknown. R threads this uncertainty through every arithmetic and comparison operation automatically, which is why `NA` feels contagious. It is. That's the point. The question is what this contagion looks like when you have 344 measurements and a handful of gaps.


### Exercises {.unnumbered}

1. What does `c(1, NA, 3) > 2` return? What happens to the `NA`?
2. How many missing values are in `readings`? (Hint: combine `sum()` and `is.na()`.)
3. What does `NA | TRUE` return? What about `NA & FALSE`? One of each pair is not `NA`. Why?


## Meet penguins {#sec-meet-penguins}

R 4.5 and later includes a dataset called `penguins` (from the Palmer Archipelago in Antarctica), and it's a good place to see everything from this chapter working on real measurements. If you're running an older version, install it with `install.packages("palmerpenguins")` and load it with `library(palmerpenguins)`.

```{r}
#| eval: false
head(penguins)
```

`penguins` is a data frame (a table), and we'll learn about those properly in @sec-data-frames. For now, the important thing is that you can pull out a single column with `$`, and that column is a vector:

```{r}
#| eval: false
bill <- penguins$bill_length_mm
```

Now you have a numeric vector of 344 bill length measurements, with some `NA`s mixed in. Everything from this chapter applies:

```{r}
#| eval: false
length(bill)
typeof(bill)
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
mean(bill, na.rm = TRUE)
range(bill, na.rm = TRUE)
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
sum(is.na(bill))
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
long_bills <- bill[bill > 50 & !is.na(bill)]
length(long_bills)
```

How many penguins have a bill longer than 50 mm? Logical indexing filters the values, `is.na()` handles missing data, and `length()` counts the result. One line each, no loops. You wrote five operations in this chapter that would each require explicit iteration in most languages; in R, the vector carried the iteration for you. Data frames, which we turn to next, are built from exactly these vectors, stacked side by side like columns in a spreadsheet. The machinery you just learned doesn't go away; it becomes the engine underneath everything else.


### Exercises {.unnumbered}

1. What is the mean body mass (`penguins$body_mass_g`) of the penguins? Don't forget the `NA`s.
2. How many penguins have a flipper length (`penguins$flipper_length_mm`) greater than 200?
3. What is the shortest bill in the dataset? (Hint: `min()` with `na.rm = TRUE`.)
